name: Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith
title: Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith (RA): The Prophet's Brother
meta_description: The story of Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith (RA), the cousin and milk-brother of the Prophet Muhammad who fought him for twenty years, then returned, was forgiven, and spent the rest of his life in the mosque.
keywords: Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith, the Prophet's brother, story of Abu Sufyan, sahaba stories, the Firsts, conquest of Makkah, forgiveness in Islam
lede: A year before the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ passed away, you might have seen a man in Madinah who looked almost exactly like him. He kept his head down. He was the first into the mosque and the last to leave. His eyes were always wet with tears, and he spoke to almost no one.

If you asked the Prophet ﷺ who that was, he would tell you: that is my brother. The answer would have surprised you, because for twenty years this same man had been one of his most determined enemies. This is the story of Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with him).

sections.h2: As close as a brother could be
paras: Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith was the Prophet's first cousin, a son of al-Harith, the eldest son of Abd al-Muttalib. His father had once dug at the well of Zamzam beside that same grandfather, throwing the dirt aside as they uncovered the water that pilgrims still drink today. From the very start, this family was bound together at the deepest level.

He was born around the same year as the Prophet ﷺ, and in that society a paternal cousin was as close as a brother. He was also nursed by the same woman who nursed the Prophet ﷺ, so he was a milk-brother too. Later he married into the family of Abu Talib, the uncle who raised the Prophet ﷺ like a father.

On top of all this, he looked strikingly like the Prophet ﷺ. He was counted among the small handful of people whose resemblance to him was remarkable. When he travelled to Syria, people who saw him would say at once: this must be the cousin, the brother, of that man, because the likeness was unmistakable.

The two of them grew up joined at the hip. They were closest friends. They gave each other gifts, attended one another's weddings, and brought presents back from their journeys, exactly the way blood brothers would.

Abu Sufyan was known for his eloquence and his fine manners. He was a poet who wrote in praise of noble tribes and noble men. People rub off on one another, and a lifetime beside the Prophet ﷺ had left its mark on him. Anyone watching their lives in Makkah would have seen two cousins as inseparable as family can be.

So when the revelation came, the Prophet ﷺ expected that this man, of all people, would be among the first to recognise the truth and stand beside him. He had shown him nothing but good his whole life. Surely his brother would see in him what others were already beginning to see. He was waiting for his support.

Instead, when the Prophet ﷺ called his own clan, Banu Hashim, to Islam, Abu Sufyan turned his back. All that closeness hardened into open hostility. The Prophet ﷺ had thought his people might run him out, but never imagined the rejection would come from those nearest to him.

Scholars who read his poetry see envy in it, and something harder to name. The two of them had been equals their whole lives, married at the same time, raising children at the same time. Now one of them was a Prophet, and that ruptured everything. There was also a bitterness that said: everything was fine, why did you bring this and complicate our lives?

It was not a forced or half-hearted enmity. It was genuine. For twenty years he used both his tongue and his sword against the Prophet ﷺ. He became the leading poet attacking him, trading verse for verse with Hassan ibn Thabit, page after page, and he did not miss a single battle against the Muslims.

Much of the harsh poetry the books attribute to a man named Abu Sufyan in those years was in fact his, not that of the more famous Abu Sufyan ibn Harb. The likeness between this brother and the Prophet ﷺ even shaped how he could be answered. When Hassan offered to reply, the Prophet ﷺ asked how he would do it without wounding him too, since insulting Abu Sufyan's family and face meant insulting his own. Hassan promised to draw the Prophet ﷺ out of it the way a hair is drawn from dough.

This pain ran so deep that the Prophet ﷺ would not speak about him and could not bring himself to look at him for years afterward. It is part of his human example: even in pain he never wronged anyone, but he did feel the hurt of a brother turned enemy.

sections.h2: The day he ran out of excuses
paras: One by one, his brothers and his sister accepted Islam. His own wife, Jumana, leaned toward it and could not understand her husband's stubbornness. By the conquest of Makkah, he was the last of them still holding out.

When the Prophet ﷺ arrived at the edge of Makkah with a great army, Abu Sufyan understood that it was over. He went to his wife and his son, unsure what to do. His wife shamed him gently: the whole world is embracing this man, and you, who should have been the first to support him, are the one still insisting on enmity. He said he was covered in shame.

He set out to find the Prophet ﷺ, wrapping his face, not knowing whether he would be killed by him or by someone else along the way. He left his camel and walked the last stretch on foot, so as not to cause any commotion. It was the posture of a man coming not to fight, but to apologise.

When he uncovered his face before the Prophet ﷺ, the Prophet turned away. He tried again to meet his eyes, and again the Prophet turned from him. There was no aggression and no harm, but there was no warmth yet either. The Muslims saw it, and their anger rose. Abu Bakr (RA) told him to leave. Umar (RA) was harsher still. A young man of the Ansar stood and rebuked him for everything he had done.

He went to the Prophet's uncle, Abbas, who interceded for him. The Prophet ﷺ said he would be forgiven, but that he could not simply pretend none of it had happened: this was the cousin who had attacked his honour. Abbas counselled patience. Ali, his brother-in-law, told him the same: give it time.

Day after day the Prophet ﷺ softened a little more, yet the old closeness had not returned. Abu Sufyan was grateful only to be forgiven, but the full look of love he longed for was not there. He told himself that if it stayed this way, he would take his son into the desert and disappear until he died, rather than remain in the presence of the man he had wronged so badly.

sections.h2: The smile he was waiting for
paras: The change finally came at the battle of Hunayn. When the Muslim army was ambushed and scattered, only a few remained around the Prophet ﷺ. Abu Sufyan was one of them. He took hold of the reins of the Prophet's mule on one side and began to fight, determined to have his back now the way he should have from the very beginning.

In the middle of the fighting the Prophet ﷺ asked who this man was, clinging to him and defending him. The answer came: your brother, Abu Sufyan. And there, at last, the Prophet ﷺ welcomed him, prayed for him, and embraced him.

He said he hoped Allah had given him this brother in place of Hamza, the beloved uncle and milk-brother whose death at Uhud had grieved him so deeply. It was a precious thing to say, and Abu Sufyan never forgot it.

sections.h2: A man who lived in the mosque
paras: Back in Madinah, the companions who had once shunned him now honoured him, because they saw how the Prophet ﷺ honoured him. But Abu Sufyan did not coast on his lineage or his new status. He set out to make up for twenty lost years.

He fell in love with the Quran and became known for it, reciting day and night. He grew quiet, almost like a hermit. He was the first into the mosque and the last to leave, praying through the heat of the long summer days, his eyes welling with tears. When Aisha (RA) asked who he was, the Prophet ﷺ told her: that is my cousin, Abu Sufyan, the first to enter the mosque and the last to leave it, and his eyes never leave his sandals.

After the Prophet ﷺ passed away, Abu Sufyan kept this life unchanged. He carried the weight of knowing he had missed the greater part of those years, with so little time to stand beside the Prophet ﷺ as a Muslim. He grieved the loss in some of the most tender poetry ever written for him, saying he wept the whole night over the greatest tragedy ever to reach the earth, and calling that the saddest night in all of history.

Near the end of his own life, while still healthy enough to recover, he went to the graveyard beside the Prophet ﷺ and dug his own grave with his own hands, asking Allah to let him follow his brother. Three days later, as his family wept around him, he told them not to cry: he had not committed a single sin since the day he became a Muslim. He was buried in the section closest to the family of the Prophet ﷺ.

lessons_heading: What Abu Sufyan's life teaches us
lessons_intro: His story is not a clean one. It is a man who got almost everything wrong for twenty years, and then spent the rest of his life getting it right. That is exactly why it reaches us.

lessons.bold: It is never too late to turn back.
lessons.text: He came at the very end, after two decades of enmity, and he was still received, still forgiven, still loved. No history is too far gone to repair.

lessons.bold: Forgiveness can be real even when it takes time.
lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ forgave him at once, yet the warmth returned slowly. Holding both is part of being human: we can pardon someone and still need time for the hurt to heal.

lessons.bold: Repentance is shown in a changed life.
lessons.text: He did not simply say the words. He gave the rest of his days to the mosque and the Quran, quietly trying to make up for the time he had lost.

lessons.bold: A quiet, sincere ending can outshine a loud past.
lessons.text: The man who once wrote poems against the Prophet ﷺ ended as the first into the mosque and the last to leave, his eyes lowered, his heart soft.

faq.q: Who was Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith?
faq.a: He was a first cousin and milk-brother of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, raised alongside him and closely resembling him. He opposed the Prophet ﷺ for about twenty years before accepting Islam at the conquest of Makkah and becoming a devoted companion.

faq.q: Is this the same Abu Sufyan who was a famous enemy of Islam?
faq.a: No. He is often confused with Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, the well-known leader of Quraysh. This Abu Sufyan, son of al-Harith, was the Prophet's own cousin and the chief poet who attacked him in verse during those years.

faq.q: How did the Prophet ﷺ receive him when he returned?
faq.a: At first the Prophet ﷺ turned away from him and would not meet his eyes, because of the deep hurt of the past. He forgave him, but the warmth returned slowly, and was fully restored at the battle of Hunayn, where Abu Sufyan fought at his side.

faq.q: What can we learn from his life?
faq.a: That it is never too late to turn back, that genuine forgiveness can still take time to heal, and that true repentance shows itself in a changed life rather than in words alone.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us carry some version of his regret: years we wish we had spent differently, a relationship we let harden, a turn toward what is good that we kept putting off. His life says the door is still open. He came late, after a long enmity, and was still welcomed, still forgiven, still given a place close to the people he loved.

There is a second gift in his story for anyone who has been hurt. The Prophet ﷺ forgave him fully, and still needed time before the old warmth came back. If you have pardoned someone but your heart is not yet healed, you are not failing. Forgive, give it time, and let the slow work happen. Carry one of those two truths into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
A year before the Prophet ﷺ passed away, a man walked into the mosque in Madinah.

Head down. First in, last out. Eyes always wet with tears.

He looked almost exactly like the Prophet ﷺ.

"Who is that?" they asked.

The Prophet ﷺ said: that is my brother.

The answer was a shock. For twenty years, this man had been his enemy.

Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith was his first cousin and his milk-brother.

They grew up joined at the hip. Closest friends. Gifts, weddings, journeys shared like blood brothers.

Then the revelation came.

The Prophet ﷺ expected him to be among the first to believe.

Instead, he turned his back. All that love became open enmity.

For twenty years he fought with both tongue and sword.

The leading poet against the Prophet ﷺ. Not a single battle missed.

The hurt ran so deep the Prophet ﷺ would not even speak of him.

At the conquest of Makkah, he was the last to hold out.

His own wife shamed him: the whole world is embracing this man, and you are still his enemy?

He came back covered in shame.

The Prophet ﷺ forgave him, but turned away from his eyes.

The warmth came slowly. It was fully restored only when Abu Sufyan fought at his side at Hunayn.

Then, at last, the embrace.

He spent the rest of his life in the mosque, in love with the Quran.

First in, last out. Before he died he dug his own grave, asking only to follow his brother.

**It is never too late to turn back.**

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
For twenty years, Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the Prophet's most determined enemies. He was also his first cousin and milk-brother, raised at his side, and he looked almost exactly like him.

When he finally came back at the conquest of Makkah, the Prophet ﷺ forgave him, but could not look at him for a time. The hurt was real. The warmth returned slowly, and was complete only when Abu Sufyan stood and fought beside him at Hunayn.

He spent the rest of his life quiet, in the mosque, in love with the Quran: first in, last out, his eyes lowered. He died saying he had not sinned since the day he accepted Islam.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/the-prophet-s-brother

email.subject: The brother who fought the Prophet for twenty years
email.preview: He looked almost exactly like the Prophet ﷺ, and for twenty years he was his enemy.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

A year before the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ passed away, you might have noticed a man in the mosque in Madinah who looked almost exactly like him. He kept his head down. He was the first to arrive and the last to leave. His eyes were always wet with tears, and he spoke to almost no one.

If you asked who he was, the Prophet ﷺ would tell you: that is my brother.

The answer would have surprised you. This was Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with him), his first cousin and milk-brother. They had grown up joined at the hip, closest friends, sharing gifts and weddings and journeys the way blood brothers do.

Then the revelation came, and Abu Sufyan turned his back. The man the Prophet ﷺ most expected to support him became one of his fiercest enemies. For twenty years he fought him with both his tongue and his sword, the leading poet against him, never missing a battle. The hurt ran so deep that the Prophet ﷺ would not even speak of him.

At the conquest of Makkah, he was the last of his family still holding out. His own wife shamed him gently: the whole world is embracing this man, and you are still his enemy? He came back covered in shame, his face wrapped, not knowing if he would be killed.

The Prophet ﷺ forgave him. But he turned away from his eyes, and the warmth returned only slowly. It was made whole at the battle of Hunayn, when Abu Sufyan took hold of the reins of his mule and fought at his side. There, at last, came the embrace.

There is a quiet lesson in this for anyone who has been hurt. Forgiveness was given at once, yet the heart still needed time. If you have pardoned someone and your heart is not yet healed, you are not failing. Forgive, and give it time.

He spent the rest of his life in the mosque, in love with the Quran, first in and last to leave. He died saying he had not sinned since the day he became a Muslim.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: A year before the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ passed away, there was a man in Madinah who looked almost exactly like him. He kept his head down. He was the first into the mosque and the last to leave, and his eyes were always wet with tears. When people asked who he was, the Prophet ﷺ said: that is my brother.

His name was Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with him). He was the Prophet's first cousin and his milk-brother, born around the same year, raised at his side. They were closest friends, giving each other gifts and sharing one another's weddings, as close as two brothers could be.

Then the revelation came, and everything broke. The Prophet ﷺ had expected his brother to be among the first to believe. Instead, Abu Sufyan turned his back, and all that love hardened into enmity. For twenty years he fought him with poems and with his sword, and the hurt ran so deep that the Prophet ﷺ would not even speak his name.

At the conquest of Makkah, Abu Sufyan was the last of his family still holding out. His own wife shamed him gently, asking how he could still be an enemy when the whole world was turning to this man. Covered in shame, he wrapped his face and came back, not knowing whether he would be killed.

The Prophet ﷺ forgave him, but he turned away from his eyes. The warmth did not return all at once. It came slowly, and was made whole only at the battle of Hunayn, when Abu Sufyan held the reins of the Prophet's mule and fought at his side. There, at last, came the embrace, and the Prophet ﷺ said he hoped Allah had given him this brother in place of the uncle he had lost.

After that, Abu Sufyan gave the rest of his life to the mosque and the Quran. He grew quiet, reciting day and night, first to arrive and last to leave, his eyes lowered to the ground. Near the end he dug his own grave with his own hands and asked Allah to let him follow his brother. He died saying he had not sinned since the day he became a Muslim, and was buried close to the family of the Prophet ﷺ.
storybook.pullquote: That is my brother, the first to enter the mosque and the last to leave it.
storybook.reflection: Abu Sufyan got almost everything wrong for twenty years, and was still welcomed back. His story holds two gifts: it is never too late to turn toward what is good, and forgiveness can be real even while the heart is still healing. If someone has hurt you, you may pardon them and still need time. Forgive, give it time, and let the slow mending happen.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
